title: Resistance mechanisms of acidophilic Chlamydomonas sp. 1710 to aluminum stress: Insights from transcriptomics, proteomics and physiology.
author_string: Yu, Yongkun, Banda, Joseph Frazer, Yu, Na, Li, Hongyi, Hao, Chunbo et al.
abstract: Acidophilic algae play a crucial role in AMD remediation. However, little is known about their survival strategy in such harsh environments. Here, we investigated the resistance mechanisms of acidophilic alga Chlamydomonas sp. 1710 to aluminum (Al), a typical metal rich in AMD, by transcriptomic, proteomic and physiological analyses. Model alga Chlamydomonas reinhardtii exhibited a negative increase when exposed to 30 mg/L Al, while Chlamydomonas sp. 1710 under 1000 mg/L Al stress decreased growth by only 18 % relative to a no-Al control. To block Al entry, C. sp. 1710 enhanced the synthesis of hydroxyproline-rich glycoproteins, the major components of cell wall, by upregulating prolyl 4-hydroxylase and glycosyltransferases while incorporating callose into cell wall and secreting exopolysaccharides around the cell. Meanwhile, the alga expelled the intracellular Al by ATP-binding cassette transporters or chelated it by ferritin and phytochelatin. To counteract the oxidative stress induced by Al, C. sp. 1710 augmented the production of both enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidants, and enhanced the incorporation of methionine into proteins through the controlled inaccuracy of methionyl-tRNA synthetase. Furthermore, it strengthened the ubiquitin-proteasome system to degrade severely damaged proteins, thereby maintaining protein homeostasis. These Al resistance mechanisms were modulated by cell signals including Ca<sup>2+</sup> and cyclic GMP.
